heroOverview: Spherophakia is a rare congenital malformation in which the crystalline lens of the eye is abnormally small, round, and spherical rather than its usual biconcave disc shape, with an equatorial diameter below about 6 mm compared with the normal 9–10 mm. The abnormally shaped lens sits on lax zonular fibres that hold it improperly in position, creating a characteristic clinical triad: lens subluxation, high lenticular myopia, and an elevated lifelong risk of pupil block glaucoma. The condition affects roughly 1–3 per 100,000 individuals and occurs most often as part of Weill–Marchesani syndrome or, less frequently, Marfan syndrome and homocystinuria, though isolated autosomal-dominant forms exist. Early recognition is critical because angle-closure glaucoma in spherophakia can occur acutely in childhood or adolescence and, if untreated, leads to irreversible optic nerve damage within hours to days.

keyStats.prevalence: Estimated at approximately 1–3 per 100,000 population; precise figures are uncertain due to underreporting
keyStats.demographics: Affects males and females; bilateral in the vast majority of cases; may be detected in childhood or not until adolescence
keyStats.avgAge: Congenital; typically diagnosed in the first decade of life when visual symptoms or glaucoma prompt evaluation
keyStats.globalCases: No reliable global case count; Weill–Marchesani syndrome, the commonest systemic association, occurs in roughly 1 per 100,000 births

definition: Spherophakia (ICD-10: Q12.4) is a congenital lens anomaly characterised by an abnormally small, spherical crystalline lens with a diameter typically below 6 mm and an increased anterior–posterior diameter giving it a nearly round cross-sectional profile on slit-lamp examination. The morphological abnormality arises during fetal lens development when the equatorial growth of the lens is curtailed, producing excess curvature and high lenticular myopia. The lens sits on zonular fibres that are lax, short, or disorganised, predisposing to subluxation — lateral displacement of the lens from the optical axis. In the natural undilated state, the relatively mobile lens can bow forward against the posterior iris surface, obstructing aqueous flow from the posterior chamber through the pupil into the anterior chamber, a mechanism called pupillary block that raises intraocular pressure acutely (angle-closure glaucoma). This mechanism distinguishes spherophakia from most other causes of glaucoma, because applying a miotic agent (pilocarpine), which would normally reduce intraocular pressure, can paradoxically worsen it by pulling the lens further forward — a trap for the unwary clinician. Spherophakia is recognised as an isolated autosomal-recessive or autosomal-dominant condition and as a feature of Weill–Marchesani syndrome (caused by ADAMTS10, LTBP2, or FBN1 mutations), Marfan syndrome, homocystinuria, Alport syndrome, and Klinefelter syndrome. Management involves a cardiologist and geneticist alongside the ophthalmologist.

typesClassification.description: The spherical lens exists without systemic abnormalities; inheritance can be autosomal dominant or recessive; the main risks are lens subluxation and glaucoma.
The commonest syndromic form; accompanied by short stature, brachydactyly, joint stiffness, and thickened skin; the systemic phenotype is opposite to the tall, lax Marfan habitus.
Less common than ectopia lentis in Marfan, spherophakia occurs when the FBN1 mutation particularly affects equatorial lens growth; zonular laxity is marked.
Elevated homocysteine disrupts zonular attachment and can cause both spherical lens shape and superior displacement; concurrent thromboembolism risk influences perioperative planning.
The most severe form, with an extremely small lens diameter below 5 mm; pupil block glaucoma risk is highest in this subgroup because the small lens easily passes into the pupillary aperture.

primarySymptoms: High myopia — the steeply curved anterior and posterior surfaces of the spherical lens act as a very powerful plus element, focusing light far in front of the retina; affected individuals often present with severe distance-vision blurring from early childhood.
Fluctuating vision — as the subluxated lens shifts with eye movements, the effective optical axis changes; vision may vary moment to moment or between different gaze directions.

Lens subluxation visible on slit-lamp — the round lens edge (equator) is visible in the undilated pupil when the lens is displaced, appearing as a dark crescent that moves with head tilting; this iridodonesis (quivering iris) is a characteristic sign.
Acute eye pain and redness — when the lens lodges in the pupil and triggers angle-closure glaucoma, the eye becomes intensely red and painful within hours, often accompanied by nausea and vomiting.
Coloured haloes around lights — a symptom of corneal oedema from acutely elevated intraocular pressure during a glaucoma episode.
Decreased visual acuity that is not fully correctable with spectacles — the abnormal lens surface curvature and subluxation produce optical aberrations that spectacles cannot fully compensate.
Strabismus — misalignment arising from anisometropia, amblyopia, or disrupted binocular fusion because of the asymmetric lenticular contribution to each eye.
Accommodative difficulties — the spherical lens with lax zonules has little capacity for normal accommodation, causing near-vision blur that is disproportionate to the degree of myopia.
Photophobia — sensitivity to light during or after a glaucoma episode, or related to corneal oedema following chronic pressure elevation.
Headache and brow ache — particularly in adolescents with intermittent angle closure, who may report recurrent one-sided head pain attributed incorrectly to migraine.
earlyWarningSigns: A child squinting or sitting very close to the television suggests high myopia caused by the spherical lens.
Iridodonesis (wobbling or shimmering of the iris with eye movements) observed by a careful clinician at slit-lamp examination, indicating a mobile subluxated lens.
An asymmetric or eccentric red reflex on examination, with one reflex shifted or showing a crescent of aphakia, suggesting lens subluxation.
A family history of Weill–Marchesani syndrome, early-onset glaucoma, or unexplained lens surgery in childhood.
emergencySigns: Sudden onset of severe eye pain, blurred vision, a red eye with a fixed mid-dilated pupil, and nausea — these are the hallmarks of acute angle-closure glaucoma from pupillary block and require same-day emergency ophthalmological management.
Rapidly declining vision in a young child who had been reading normally, combined with eye redness and tearing, demanding urgent review.

Any episode that resembles a migraine aura with visual disturbance and one-sided head pain in a child known to have spherophakia, which should prompt intraocular pressure measurement to exclude glaucoma rather than empirical migraine treatment.

causes.description: ADAMTS10 encodes a secreted metalloprotease that processes fibrillin microfibrils in the lens zonule and sclera; biallelic loss-of-function mutations disrupt equatorial lens growth and zonular attachment, producing the short round lens with lax zonules.

causes.cause: FBN1 mutations (Marfan syndrome or isolated dominant spherophakia)
causes.description: Fibrillin-1 is the structural protein of zonular fibres; certain FBN1 mutations prevent normal zonule elongation, limiting equatorial lens growth while increasing anterior curvature and producing a spherical profile.

LTBP2 (latent TGF-beta binding protein 2) anchors TGF-beta to the extracellular matrix; mutations cause autosomal-recessive Weill–Marchesani or isolated microspherophakia by altering fibrillin-rich scaffold structure in the lens.

Elevated plasma homocysteine from cystathionine beta-synthase deficiency damages the disulfide bridges of fibrillin, causing progressive zonular dissolution; the lens becomes increasingly spherical as it migrates toward the pupil.

In some pedigrees spherophakia segregates as an autosomal-dominant trait without systemic features; the gene has not been fully characterised but likely encodes a structural or signalling component of the equatorial growth zone.

Klinefelter syndrome (47,XXY) and Alport syndrome (COL4A mutations) have been reported in association with spherophakia; the mechanism in these conditions likely involves collagen and extracellular matrix disruption in the lens equatorial region.

riskFactors.description: Autosomal-recessive inheritance; parental consanguinity doubles the risk; siblings of an affected child carry a 25% risk; the systemic phenotype of short stature and joint stiffness helps distinguish it clinically.
Autosomal-dominant inheritance with 50% transmission per pregnancy; tall stature, arachnodactyly, and aortic root dilation accompany the ocular features.
Autosomal recessive; neonatal metabolic screening detects elevated methionine or homocysteine; untreated, the zonular damage progresses and thromboembolism risk is high.

riskFactors.factor: Family history of early lens dislocation or juvenile glaucoma
riskFactors.description: A first-degree relative with unexplained lens surgery or glaucoma in childhood suggests a heritable lens-zonule disorder warranting ophthalmic examination of all children in the family.
Raises the probability of autosomal-recessive causes (ADAMTS10, LTBP2, CBS) substantially; screening of all offspring is indicated when consanguinity is identified.
Some series report earlier and more severe angle-closure glaucoma in male patients with Weill–Marchesani syndrome, possibly related to a smaller anterior chamber.
A naturally shallow anterior chamber gives the subluxated spherical lens less space to manoeuvre before engaging the pupil; eyes with shallow chambers are at higher risk of pupil block glaucoma.

affectedDemographics: Children and adolescents presenting with unexplained severe myopia
Individuals with Weill–Marchesani syndrome (short stature, brachydactyly)
Individuals with Marfan syndrome (tall stature, arachnodactyly)
Individuals with homocystinuria detected by newborn screening or later
Families with consanguineous unions in populations where ADAMTS10 or LTBP2 variants are more prevalent
diagnosisOverview: Spherophakia is often suspected when a child or adolescent presents with unusually high myopia and, on slit-lamp examination, shows iridodonesis, a visible round lens equator in the pupillary zone, or a lens that shifts with changes in head position. The diagnosis is confirmed at slit-lamp biomicroscopy: the spherical lens profile is visible in cross-section as a small, round disc with an anterior–posterior diameter nearly equal to the equatorial diameter, in contrast to the normal flat biconcave disc. Cycloplegic refraction quantifies the lenticular myopic contribution, which typically ranges from −10 to −25 D. Gonioscopy of the anterior chamber angle assesses whether angle closure is present or the angle is occludable. Intraocular pressure measurement is essential at every visit because subclinical intermittent pressure spikes may cause optic nerve damage before a dramatic acute presentation. Ultrasonographic biomicroscopy (UBM) or anterior-segment optical coherence tomography (AS-OCT) documents the anterior segment geometry, lens thickness, and zonular defects precisely. Systemic evaluation follows: clinical examination for the Marfan habitus (tall, arachnodactyly, hyperlaxity) or Weill–Marchesani habitus (short stature, brachydactyly, stiff joints); echocardiography for aortic root in Marfan; plasma homocysteine; and chromosomal microarray with gene-panel testing covering ADAMTS10, LTBP2, FBN1, and CBS. Ophthalmologists must remember not to use pilocarpine without first ruling out pupil block, as this miotic agent causes the ciliary muscle to relax the zonules further, pushing the spherical lens forward and worsening the block.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Directly visualises the spherical lens profile, quantifies subluxation, detects iridodonesis, and assesses anterior-segment anatomy including corneal status and anterior chamber depth.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: The patient rests their chin and forehead against supports while a narrow beam of light is used to examine the eye; takes 10–15 minutes; completely painless.

diagnosticTests.purpose: After pupil dilation and cycloplegia, refraction accurately quantifies the high lenticular myopia without accommodation masking the full refractive error.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Drops dilate pupils and relax accommodation for several hours; a second refraction is performed 30–45 minutes later; near vision is blurry for several hours.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Visualises the anterior chamber angle using a special mirrored contact lens to determine whether the angle is open or closed, a critical step in understanding glaucoma risk.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A small contact lens is placed on the numbed eye with a drop of gel; takes about 5 minutes; mild pressure sensation only.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects elevated pressure from intermittent or chronic angle closure before permanent optic nerve damage occurs.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A small probe briefly touches the numbed cornea (applanation tonometry) or a puff of air is directed at the eye (non-contact); takes seconds; no discomfort with anaesthetic drops.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Provides high-resolution imaging of the anterior segment to document lens thickness, zonular defects, iris-lens contact, and angle configuration, especially useful in eyes with hazy media.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: UBM uses a water bath immersion technique with an ultrasound probe; AS-OCT is a non-contact scan similar to a photograph; both take 15–20 minutes.

diagnosticTests.test: Plasma homocysteine and genetic panel (ADAMTS10, LTBP2, FBN1, CBS)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies the molecular cause, confirms or excludes homocystinuria (which changes perioperative management due to thromboembolism risk), and guides cascade family testing.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Fasting blood sample; results from a metabolic and genetics laboratory typically take 2–8 weeks.

treatmentOverview: Treatment of spherophakia is dictated by whether glaucoma is present or threatened, and by the degree of lens subluxation. For eyes without angle closure and with correctable vision, optical management — spectacles or rigid contact lenses that correct the high lenticular myopia — is the first step, combined with regular tonometry and gonioscopy surveillance. Peripheral iridotomy (a laser procedure that creates an opening in the iris) prevents pupil block by allowing aqueous to bypass the lens–pupil interface and flow directly into the anterior chamber; it is recommended prophylactically in all eyes with a narrow occludable angle. Miotic agents such as pilocarpine are contraindicated in spherophakia as they relax zonules and push the lens forward, worsening the block. When glaucoma develops, intraocular pressure is first managed with aqueous suppressant drops (beta-blockers, alpha-agonists, carbonic anhydrase inhibitors). Definitive treatment for progressive or uncontrollable disease is lens extraction — removal of the abnormally shaped lens eliminates both the refractive problem and the pupil block mechanism simultaneously. Lensectomy in spherophakia is technically demanding because the zonules are fragile and the anterior capsule requires a careful circular capsulorhexis; vitrectomy may be combined if zonules rupture. After lens removal, an intraocular lens implant is placed and strong spectacles or contact lenses compensate for the loss of natural accommodation. For Weill–Marchesani and Marfan syndrome, systemic management of the underlying condition runs in parallel with ocular care.

medicalTreatments.name: Spectacle or contact lens correction
medicalTreatments.description: High-power minus spectacle lenses or rigid gas-permeable contact lenses correct the extreme lenticular myopia; contact lenses are preferred when anisometropia or optical aberrations from subluxation make spectacle correction difficult.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Optical correction resolves refractive amblyopia in approximately 40–55% of children when initiated in infancy and combined with patching; spectacle correction alone does not alter the structural course of the disease.

medicalTreatments.name: Laser peripheral iridotomy (LPI)
medicalTreatments.description: A neodymium-YAG laser creates a small hole through the peripheral iris, providing an alternative aqueous flow route that bypasses the lens–pupil interface and eliminates the pupil block mechanism before acute angle closure occurs.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces the risk of acute angle-closure glaucoma episodes by approximately 60–80% in eyes with an occludable angle; most effective when performed prophylactically before any angle-closure episode.

medicalTreatments.name: Topical aqueous suppressants (timolol, brimonidine, dorzolamide)
medicalTreatments.description: When intraocular pressure is elevated, drops reduce aqueous production to protect the optic nerve while definitive surgical management is planned; beta-blockers and carbonic anhydrase inhibitors are first-line agents in this setting.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduce intraocular pressure by 20–30% from baseline; adequate long-term pressure control without surgery is achieved in approximately 40–60% of cases before lens extraction becomes necessary.

medicalTreatments.name: Mydriatic-cycloplegic therapy (atropine, cyclopentolate)
medicalTreatments.description: Paradoxically, dilating the pupil — by relaxing the sphincter — allows the spherical lens to fall posteriorly away from the pupillary aperture, relieving acute pupil block when a laser or surgical iridotomy is not immediately available; used as a temporising measure only.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Breaks acute pupil block in approximately 50–70% of cases as an emergency temporising measure; must be followed by definitive iridotomy or lensectomy.

surgicalOptions.name: Lensectomy with intraocular lens implantation
surgicalOptions.description: Surgical removal of the spherical lens via phacoemulsification or vitrectomy, followed by placement of a sutured or scleral-fixated intraocular lens to restore optical focus; eliminates both the pupil block mechanism and the extreme lenticular myopia simultaneously.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Intraocular pressure normalisation achieved in approximately 70–85% of cases after surgery; visual acuity of 20/40 or better in approximately 60–75% in series where surgery is performed before advanced glaucomatous damage.

surgicalOptions.name: Combined lensectomy with anterior vitrectomy
surgicalOptions.description: When zonular fragility causes lens instability during surgery or when vitreous presents anteriorly, anterior vitrectomy is added to remove vitreous and prevent its incarceration in the wound.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Reduces intraoperative complications from zonular rupture by approximately 30–40% compared with lens surgery alone in fragile-zonule eyes; overall surgical success rate similar to isolated lensectomy.

surgicalOptions.name: Trabeculectomy or glaucoma drainage device for refractory glaucoma
surgicalOptions.description: When intraocular pressure remains uncontrolled after lens extraction, a filtering bleb (trabeculectomy) or a glaucoma drainage device lowers pressure by creating an alternate outflow pathway.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Single-procedure success (IOP controlled without drops at 1 year) of approximately 60–75% for trabeculectomy; drainage devices achieve slightly lower initial rates but more durable outcomes in longer follow-up series.

alternativeTreatments.name: Low-vision rehabilitation
alternativeTreatments.description: For children who retain imperfect vision despite surgery, low-vision specialists prescribe magnification, tinted lenses, and lighting adaptations to maximise functional independence in daily life and learning.

alternativeTreatments.name: Genetic counselling and metabolic management
alternativeTreatments.description: In homocystinuria, dietary methionine restriction and betaine supplementation correct the enzymatic defect, reducing zonular damage progression and the catastrophic thromboembolism risk; pyridoxine responsiveness testing should be performed in all newly diagnosed patients.

whySeeSpecialist: Spherophakia requires a paediatric ophthalmologist experienced in congenital lens anomalies and angle-closure glaucoma management, combined with clinical genetics input. The risk of acute angle-closure glaucoma — potentially permanent blindness within hours if missed — makes timely specialist referral non-negotiable. Pilocarpine, which a non-specialist might instinctively prescribe for angle closure, is contraindicated and can precipitate a crisis.
doctorSelectionGuide: Look for a paediatric ophthalmologist with subspecialty experience in paediatric glaucoma and complex lens surgery, ideally at a tertiary centre where an ultrasonographic biomicroscope is available for anterior-segment assessment. A clinical geneticist should evaluate all bilateral or syndromic cases. In homocystinuria, a metabolic dietitian and haematologist experienced in perioperative anticoagulation are part of the team.
preventionStrategies: Enrol newborns in metabolic screening programmes that detect homocystinuria, allowing treatment before irreversible zonular damage occurs.
Perform genetic testing and offer ophthalmic screening to all first-degree relatives of a confirmed spherophakia patient.
Perform prophylactic laser peripheral iridotomy in all spherophakic eyes with a narrow occludable angle, before an acute glaucoma episode.
Avoid prescribing pilocarpine or other miotics to patients with spherophakia or suspected pupil block.
Ensure all patients with Weill–Marchesani or Marfan syndrome have ophthalmic examination from birth or the time of diagnosis.
lifestyleModifications: Wear the prescribed optical correction consistently to prevent amblyopia deepening during the visual critical period.
Attend pressure monitoring appointments regularly — at least every 3–6 months — even when no symptoms are present.
Seek immediate emergency ophthalmological review for any acute eye pain, redness, or visual blur — do not wait.
Avoid activities with intense Valsalva manoeuvres if the lens is significantly subluxated, as sudden pressure changes can precipitate lens dislocation.
In homocystinuria, adhere strictly to the low-methionine diet and supplements to slow zonular deterioration and reduce thrombosis risk.

recommended: Low-methionine diet and betaine supplementation in homocystinuria to reduce plasma homocysteine
Good hydration to reduce thromboembolism risk in homocystinuria
Pyridoxine (vitamin B6) supplementation in responsive homocystinuria patients — dose determined by metabolic specialist
avoid: High-methionine foods (large meat portions, eggs, dairy in homocystinuria) — worsens enzymatic deficit
Pilocarpine or miotic eye drops in spherophakia — contraindicated due to risk of worsening pupil block
Vitamin A megadosing supplements not prescribed by a clinician

exerciseGuidelines: Full physical activity is generally safe for children whose angle closure has been treated with laser iridotomy and whose intraocular pressure is controlled. Contact sports and heavy weightlifting should be avoided in individuals with significant lens subluxation until lensectomy has been performed, to reduce the risk of acute lens dislocation. In Marfan and Weill–Marchesani syndrome, systemic activity restrictions guided by cardiology and rheumatology take precedence.
dailyManagement: Take topical glaucoma drops at the same time each day if prescribed and bring any new bottle to each appointment.
Attend all scheduled slit-lamp and pressure checks — glaucoma damage can accumulate silently without symptoms.
Report new episodes of eye pain, redness, or sudden blurred vision immediately, treating them as potential glaucoma emergencies.
Wear spectacle correction all waking hours and update spectacles as prescribed when the refraction changes.
In homocystinuria, take all metabolic medications and follow dietary restrictions without interruption, particularly around any surgical procedures.
prognosis: The prognosis of spherophakia depends critically on whether glaucoma is diagnosed and treated before significant optic nerve damage accumulates. Eyes in which acute angle-closure glaucoma has been prevented by timely peripheral iridotomy or early lensectomy generally retain good functional vision if the optic nerve was intact at the time of intervention. When glaucoma is detected late or inadequately treated, permanent visual field loss and reduced acuity result. After successful lensectomy with intraocular lens implantation, intraocular pressure normalises in 70–85% of cases and visual acuity reaches 20/40 or better in approximately 60–75% of series, though this varies with the extent of pre-existing glaucomatous damage. The systemic prognosis depends on the underlying condition: Marfan syndrome and homocystinuria both carry life-threatening vascular risks that dominate the long-term outlook and require vigilant multidisciplinary care.
recoveryTimeline: After laser peripheral iridotomy, the eye recovers within 24–48 hours and intraocular pressure typically normalises within days. After lensectomy, the eye heals over 4–6 weeks; strong spectacles or contact lenses are fitted once the cornea is stable, usually at 4–6 weeks post-operatively. Vision continues to improve as the visual cortex adapts over the following months, particularly in younger children.
complications: Acute angle-closure glaucoma from pupillary block — the most dangerous complication; intraocular pressure can rise above 50 mmHg within hours, causing irreversible optic nerve damage if not relieved immediately.
Progressive chronic glaucoma — sustained pressure elevation from repeated subclinical angle closure silently damages the optic nerve, leading to peripheral and eventually central visual field loss.

Lens dislocation into the anterior chamber or vitreous — when zonules fail completely, the spherical lens can dislocate, causing acute uveitis and secondary glaucoma requiring urgent surgical removal.
Amblyopia — untreated high myopia or anisometropia during the critical developmental period causes irreversible cortical amblyopia that limits the ultimate visual acuity.

Surgical complications of lensectomy — including zonular rupture, vitreous prolapse, and posterior capsule rupture; these occur more often than in routine cataract surgery and require an experienced surgeon.
Thromboembolic events in homocystinuria — surgery and anaesthesia dramatically elevate clotting risk; perioperative anticoagulation and intravenous hydration are mandatory.

confusedWithConditions.name: Congenital glaucoma (buphthalmos)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Congenital glaucoma produces a large, hazy cornea and a globe enlarged by raised pressure from birth; spherophakia has a normal-sized globe with a small round lens and normal or intermittently elevated pressure.

confusedWithConditions.name: Marfan syndrome ectopia lentis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: In Marfan syndrome the lens is typically subluxated superiorly with a near-normal shape; in spherophakia the lens is small and round with a higher risk of pupil block glaucoma.

confusedWithConditions.name: Coloboma of the lens
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Lens coloboma produces a notched lens equator from incomplete fissure closure, with a normal spherical profile; spherophakia has a uniformly spherical lens without a notch.

relatedConditions.name: Congenital glaucoma
relatedConditions.relevance: Secondary angle-closure glaucoma is the most serious complication of spherophakia and requires shared management by a glaucoma specialist.

relatedConditions.name: Marfan syndrome (ocular)
relatedConditions.relevance: Marfan syndrome is an important systemic cause of spherophakia, requiring concurrent cardiovascular surveillance.

relatedConditions.name: Coloboma of the lens
relatedConditions.relevance: Lens structural anomalies share the genetic assessment and amblyopia management pathway with spherophakia.

relatedConditions.name: Microphthalmos
relatedConditions.relevance: Both are congenital structural eye malformations diagnosed in childhood with overlapping genetic evaluation protocols.

relatedConditions.name: Congenital cataract
relatedConditions.relevance: Cataract can co-occur inside the spherical lens, requiring combined management of the refractive and opacity problems.

faqs.question: What is spherophakia?
faqs.answer: Spherophakia is a congenital malformation in which the crystalline lens inside the eye is abnormally small and round instead of disc-shaped. The spherical lens causes high myopia and sits on weak fibres that allow it to tilt or shift, raising the risk of acute angle-closure glaucoma.

faqs.question: Is spherophakia dangerous?
faqs.answer: The main danger is acute angle-closure glaucoma, which occurs when the round lens blocks the drainage of fluid through the pupil. This causes intraocular pressure to rise rapidly, producing severe eye pain, redness, and blurred vision within hours. Without urgent treatment, permanent optic nerve damage results.

faqs.question: What causes spherophakia?
faqs.answer: Common causes are Weill–Marchesani syndrome (ADAMTS10 or LTBP2 gene mutations), Marfan syndrome (FBN1 mutation), homocystinuria (CBS mutation), and isolated autosomal-dominant or recessive inheritance. The underlying problem is weak or absent zonular fibres that fail to stretch and flatten the lens during its fetal development.

faqs.question: Can spherophakia be treated?
faqs.answer: Yes. Prophylactic laser peripheral iridotomy prevents pupil block glaucoma. Spectacles or contact lenses correct the high myopia. When glaucoma is present or the lens is severely subluxated, surgical removal of the spherical lens and replacement with an intraocular implant provides definitive treatment.

faqs.question: Why is pilocarpine dangerous in spherophakia?
faqs.answer: Pilocarpine is a miotic drop normally used to lower intraocular pressure. In spherophakia it relaxes the zonular fibres, allowing the already mobile spherical lens to move further forward into the pupil. This worsens pupil block and can precipitate or deepen an acute glaucoma crisis.

faqs.question: What is Weill–Marchesani syndrome?
faqs.answer: Weill–Marchesani syndrome is a rare connective-tissue disorder caused by ADAMTS10 or LTBP2 mutations. It produces short stature, short fingers, stiff joints, and spherophakia with microspherophakia. Unlike Marfan syndrome — which causes a tall, loose-jointed phenotype — Weill–Marchesani produces the opposite body type.

faqs.question: What is a laser peripheral iridotomy?
faqs.answer: Laser peripheral iridotomy (LPI) is a quick office procedure in which a laser creates a small hole in the peripheral iris. This opening allows aqueous fluid to flow around the lens rather than through the pupil, eliminating the pupil block mechanism and reducing the risk of acute angle-closure glaucoma.

faqs.question: How is spherophakia diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis is made at slit-lamp examination, where the small round lens profile and lens subluxation are directly visible. Cycloplegic refraction documents the extreme myopia. Gonioscopy assesses angle closure risk. Genetic testing identifies the underlying gene variant and any associated syndrome.

faqs.question: What myopia correction is used for spherophakia?
faqs.answer: Spectacle lenses with very high minus power (often −10 to −25 D) or rigid gas-permeable contact lenses correct the lenticular myopia. After surgical lens removal, strong spectacles or contact lenses — or an intraocular implant — replace the optical power of the removed lens.

faqs.question: Is spherophakia inherited?
faqs.answer: It can be. Weill–Marchesani syndrome follows autosomal-recessive inheritance (25% risk per pregnancy) or autosomal-dominant in some families. Marfan syndrome is autosomal dominant (50% risk). Isolated spherophakia can be dominant or recessive. Genetic counselling clarifies the specific recurrence risk.

faqs.question: What happens if spherophakia is untreated?
faqs.answer: Without treatment, repeated or sustained angle-closure glaucoma progressively destroys optic nerve fibres, causing permanent visual field loss and, if untreated for long enough, blindness. Severe lens subluxation can progress to complete dislocation, which causes acute pain and uveitis requiring emergency surgery.

faqs.question: Does spherophakia affect both eyes?
faqs.answer: Yes, in the vast majority of cases spherophakia is bilateral. Both eyes carry a risk of glaucoma and require monitoring. The degree of subluxation and angle closure may differ between eyes, so each is managed individually based on its specific anatomy.

faqs.question: Can children with spherophakia go to mainstream school?
faqs.answer: Most children with spherophakia can attend mainstream school with appropriate spectacles, contact lenses, and visual accommodations such as large-print materials and front seating. Once glaucoma is controlled and optical correction is in place, there is usually no barrier to standard educational participation.

faqs.question: What is the surgery for spherophakia like?
faqs.answer: Lensectomy involves removing the spherical lens under general anaesthesia in children. The surgeon makes small incisions, removes the lens with ultrasound energy or vitreous cutter instruments, and places an intraocular implant. Because the zonules are fragile, the procedure is more technically demanding than standard cataract surgery.

faqs.question: How often does glaucoma occur with spherophakia?
faqs.answer: Glaucoma is reported in approximately 50–80% of spherophakia patients over their lifetime in published series, underscoring the importance of regular intraocular pressure monitoring from the time of diagnosis, regardless of symptoms.

faqs.question: Can the vision lost from glaucoma be recovered?
faqs.answer: No. Glaucomatous optic nerve damage is irreversible. The goal of treatment is to prevent further loss rather than restore what has been lost. This is why early diagnosis, prophylactic iridotomy, and regular pressure monitoring are so important.

faqs.question: Is there a diet for spherophakia?
faqs.answer: Diet is only relevant when spherophakia is caused by homocystinuria. In that case a methionine-restricted diet with betaine and pyridoxine supplementation lowers plasma homocysteine, slowing zonular damage and reducing the thromboembolism risk that makes surgery hazardous.

faqs.question: What should I do if my child has sudden eye pain?
faqs.answer: Treat sudden severe eye pain, redness, blurred vision, and nausea in a child with spherophakia as a potential acute angle-closure glaucoma emergency. Go to an emergency ophthalmology service immediately — do not wait for a routine appointment, and do not instil pilocarpine drops at home.

faqs.question: What is the long-term outlook for someone with spherophakia?
faqs.answer: With early diagnosis, prophylactic laser iridotomy, and surgery when needed, most patients retain good functional vision. The long-term outlook depends on preventing glaucoma damage and managing any systemic syndrome — especially the cardiovascular risks of Marfan syndrome and homocystinuria.

metaDescription: Spherophakia is a congenital round-lens malformation causing high myopia and glaucoma risk. Learn causes, diagnosis, laser iridotomy and surgery.
